agent_raw:
The promotion benchmark has four flaws that prevent a fair comparison between the LSTM and the transformer.

The most fundamental problem is endpoint misalignment. The incumbent LSTM was built for 24-hour severe-sepsis screening and its published performance numbers come from that task. The transformer is optimized for 12-hour vasopressor-escalation onset — a narrower, more specific target. Comparing AUROC and recall across these two different prediction tasks attributes the performance gap to model architecture when some or all of it may reflect endpoint difficulty differences.

The second problem is timestamping inconsistency. The LSTM study protocol used EHR-charted lab timestamps. The refreshed registry uses specimen collection time when the LIS provides it. Specimen collection typically precedes charted result time by variable amounts. Features that the transformer sees "before" the prediction horizon may differ from those the LSTM protocol allowed in ways that are not controlled for.

Third, the cohort unit changed. The LSTM used individual admission records from the legacy ADT feed. The transformer uses merged care episodes that span unit transfers. Merging transfers changes which patients are counted and how their trajectory is represented, making the denominator different across the two models.

Fourth, encounter-level random splitting in a five-year multi-admission ICU dataset puts the same patients in both training and holdout, inflating the transformer's holdout estimates. A patient-level chronological split is the appropriate method for a multi-year warehouse with recurring patients.

The transformer has promising external validation numbers and the external cohort was prespecified at adequate power. These results are worth pursuing, but the benchmark should be rebuilt under harmonized conditions before promotion is authorized.
all_issues_raised: Endpoint definition changed between LSTM (24h severe-sepsis) and transformer (12h vasopressor onset)
Lab timestamp policy changed from EHR-charted to specimen collection time
Encounter-level random split leaks recurring ICU patients across train/holdout
Cohort unit changed from individual admissions to merged cross-transfer episodes

empirical_test.condition: Rebuild shared cohort with one endpoint, one timestamp policy, one cohort construction rule, and patient-level chronological split; rerun both models
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Transformer AUROC edge falls below 0.01 and alert-rate-matched recall advantage disappears on both hospitals after harmonized rerun
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Transformer still leads by at least 0.02 AUROC and at least 5 recall points at same alert rate on both internal and Hospital B cohorts under harmonized protocol
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Harmonized rerun preserves only modest internal gain, or keeps AUROC gains while erasing recall gains
